publications.title: The Overrepresented Winner? Ethnic Minority Legislators in China's National People's Congress
publications.abstract: Does increasing descriptive representation enhance substantive representation? While ethnic minorities are generally underrepresented in legislatures worldwide, they are overrepresented in China's National People's Congress. We show that overrepresentation of ethnic legislators does not result in greater substantive representation. Compared to their Han counterparts, ethnic minority legislators are less likely to sponsor bills, show no significant difference in submitting policy suggestions, and are reticent to propose legislation on ethnic issues. They face challenges in mobilizing sufficient signatures for bill sponsorship within delegations and in building cross-delegation policy coalitions. Ethnic fragmentation may hinder their ability to advance the policy agenda. Our findings suggest that institutional constraints and collective action problems may hinder ethnic legislators from achieving substantive representation.

publications.title: Can the Opening of High-Speed Rail Promote Household Multidimensional Relative Poverty Alleviation?
publications.authors: Yaning Hu, Haiyan Lu, Hao Chai, Xinyang Gao
publications.abstract: Addressing poverty is paramount, aligning with the first Sustainable Development Goal focused on eradicating poverty in all its forms. While the effects of high-speed rail (HSR) on absolute poverty have been documented, its impact on relative poverty remains understudied. This paper examines the influence of HSR on household relative poverty in China through a quasi-experimental design. The main results are as follows: (1) The opening of HSR significantly reduced the household relative poverty by approximately 1.8%. (2) This alleviation effect primarily transpires through the expansion of economic activities and employment opportunities. (3) Notably, the impact of HSR is more pronounced in lower-ranked, smaller cities and in the western regions of China. Moreover, households with migrant workers or those engaged in non-agricultural sectors derive greater benefits from HSR developments. Our results suggest that HSR opening could have contributed to China's relative poverty alleviation.

publications.title: Nationalism and War: A Macro-Comparative Perspective
publications.authors: Andreas Wimmer, Xinyang Gao (Translated), Duancheng Yang (Proofread)

workingPapers.title: Looks Like a Representative? Facial Appearances and Legislative Performance in China
workingPapers.authors: Xinyang Gao, Weilin Xiao, Songpo Yang
workingPapers.abstract: Voters and political selectors reward candidates who look the part, on a tacit assumption that those who look like able representatives will go on to represent. We argue that this translation is conditional, not automatic. An appearance advantage at selection carries into office only where holding office rewards visible effort. China's Local People's Congresses, whose deputies are indirectly elected and answer upward rather than to voters, are a setting where those incentives are weak. Scoring the faces of nearly five thousand provincial deputies with a deep-learning model that rates them as human observers would, we ask whether favorable facial traits predict the suggestions they file. They do not. Deputies who look more competent, trustworthy, attractive, or aggressive are no more active or responsive than their peers, and the most competent-looking file somewhat fewer. Appearance, we conclude, is an unreliable guide to the substance of representation: voters and selectors who read capability from a face are apt to be misled, whether under open elections or under appointment, and do better to weigh records than looks. The look of a representative is not the work of one.

workingPapers.title: “Iron Girls” Go Home: Socialist Institutional Legacies and the Gendered Costs of Market Transition in China
workingPapers.abstract: How do socialist institutional legacies shape the distributional consequences of regime transition? While modernization theory predicts that markets reduce ascriptive inequality, post-communist transitions witness paradoxical regression in female labor force participation. We propose an institutional dependence theory: because gender equality under state socialism was sustained by administrative fiat rather than endogenous development, deeper welfare-state penetration left women dependent on institutional protection and vulnerable to state retreat. We test this argument using China's 1990s marketization reform, leveraging the “Third Front Construction” campaign as a natural experiment for exogenous variation in pre-reform danwei (work unit) intensity. Using difference-in-differences and instrumental variable designs on county-level census data (1982–2000), we find that regions with extensive danwei legacies experienced significantly larger widening of the gender labor gap, driven by discriminatory public-sector displacement and re-familialization of care work, moderated by competing cultural legacies. These findings challenge linear modernization narratives, highlighting how socialist institutional legacies condition the gendered costs of market transition.

workingPapers.title: Augmenting Research Ideation with Data: An Empirical Investigation in Social Science
workingPapers.authors: Xiao Liu, Xinyi Dong, Xinyang Gao, Yansong Feng, Xun Pang
workingPapers.abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong potential for generating novel research ideas, yet such ideas often struggle with feasibility and effectiveness. In this paper, we investigate whether augmenting LLMs with relevant data during the ideation process can improve idea quality. Our framework integrates data at two stages: (1) incorporating metadata during idea generation to guide models toward more feasible concepts, and (2) introducing an automated preliminary validation step during idea selection to assess the empirical plausibility of hypotheses within ideas. We evaluate our approach in the social science domain, with a specific focus on climate negotiation topics. Expert evaluation shows that metadata improves the feasibility of generated ideas by 20%, while automated validation improves the overall quality of selected ideas by 7%. Beyond assessing the quality of LLM-generated ideas, we conduct a human study to examine whether these ideas, augmented with related data and preliminary validation, can inspire researchers in their own ideation. Participants report that the LLM-generated ideas and validation are highly useful, and the ideas they propose with such support are proven to be of higher quality than those proposed without assistance. Our findings highlight the potential of data-augmented research ideation and underscore the practical value of LLM-assisted ideation in real-world academic settings.
